coredata.dc:title: Flexible N-doped MXene quantum dot–biopolymer films with antibacterial and antioxidant functions for active food packaging

coredata.dc:description: The incorporation of nanoparticles into packaging materials offers an effective strategy for extending the shelf life of food and maintaining its freshness. In this study, we explore the potential of MXene-based quantum dots (MQDs) for advanced food packaging applications. A flexible, biodegradable thin film was fabricated by integrating MQDs into thermoplastic chitosan (TPC) using a wet chemical blending approach. The resulting nanocomposite film exhibits excellent UV resistance (>90 %), antioxidant activity (>78 %), and mechanical flexibility, making it a promising candidate for sustainable and high-performance food packaging solutions. these hybrid films based on TPC and MQDs are also resistant. The films also have been tested for mechanical strength (4–5 MPa in uniaxial tensile), and their flexibility at low temperatures was determined by measuring the glass transition temperature (Tg) at conditions below ambient (∼-30°C). Moreover, the incorporation of MQDs into chitosan films resulted in a significant reduction in oxygen and moisture permeability compared to neat chitosan films, a key characteristic for effective packaging materials. The hybrid films demonstrated a time-dependent biodegradation, with approximately 75 % weight loss after three weeks in a simulated biodegradation environment. Additionally, the films exhibited notable antioxidant and antibacterial properties, enhancing their potential for food packaging applications. Importantly, the films maintained long-term stability, with no chemical release from the MQDs incorporated into the TPC matrices, further highlighting their promising future as biodegradable, antioxidant, and antibacterial food packaging solutions.

head.ce:title: Flexible N-doped MXene quantum dot–biopolymer films with antibacterial and antioxidant functions for active food packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The incorporation of nanoparticles into packaging materials offers an effective strategy for extending the shelf life of food and maintaining its freshness. In this study, we explore the potential of MXene-based quantum dots (MQDs) for advanced food packaging applications. A flexible, biodegradable thin film was fabricated by integrating MQDs into thermoplastic chitosan (TPC) using a wet chemical blending approach. The resulting nanocomposite film exhibits excellent UV resistance (>90 %), antioxidant activity (>78 %), and mechanical flexibility, making it a promising candidate for sustainable and high-performance food packaging solutions. these hybrid films based on TPC and MQDs are also resistant. The films also have been tested for mechanical strength (4–5 MPa in uniaxial tensile), and their flexibility at low temperatures was determined by measuring the glass transition temperature (Tg) at conditions below ambient (∼-30°C). Moreover, the incorporation of MQDs into chitosan films resulted in a significant reduction in oxygen and moisture permeability compared to neat chitosan films, a key characteristic for effective packaging materials. The hybrid films demonstrated a time-dependent biodegradation, with approximately 75 % weight loss after three weeks in a simulated biodegradation environment. Additionally, the films exhibited notable antioxidant and antibacterial properties, enhancing their potential for food packaging applications. Importantly, the films maintained long-term stability, with no chemical release from the MQDs incorporated into the TPC matrices, further highlighting their promising future as biodegradable, antioxidant, and antibacterial food packaging solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable packaging ensures the long-term preservation of environmental resources by preventing the deterioration of food products, minimizing waste [1], and safeguarding food until it is used [2]. Intelligent packaging is a novel concept in the packaging industry that allows for the monitoring of the condition of packaged food and its surroundings [3]. Presently, nondegradable plastics such as polypropylene (PE), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) are extensively employed in food packaging due to their cost-effectiveness, advantageous mechanical strength, and desirable ability to act as barriers [4]. Excessive utilization of non-biodegradable plastics has led to significant carbon emissions and the emergence of severe "white pollution", posing a threat to both the environment and public health [5,6]. With the rise in urban population, there has been a surge in food demand, necessitating the implementation of effective food packaging strategies to maximize food preservation [7]. Traditionally, plastic-based materials have been utilized for these functions; nevertheless, once they were utilized, they were frequently discarded without any subsequent utilization [8]. Since 2018, the European Commission has authorized a regulation aimed to restrict or minimize the use of plastic packaging. As a result, there has been a growing focus on biobased and/or biodegradable materials, such as natural biopolymers like chitosan, hydroxypropyl cellulose, and alginate [9,10]. To alleviate the adverse effects, it is imperative to promptly substitute conventional plastic packaging with more environmentally friendly alternatives. Biodegradable and active packaging films made from natural biopolymers are becoming increasingly popular due to their numerous advantages. These films effectively protect food from external factors such as light, moisture, air, and microbes, thereby preventing spoilage and extending shelf life [11]. Active packaging systems utilizing biopolymers infused with active ingredients, such as nanomaterials, have emerged as a promising alternative to non-biodegradable plastics [12]. The packaging primarily consists of two fundamental elements: an active layer, which contains active compounds such as antimicrobials, antioxidants, and gas emitters/scavengers, and a barrier made of a polymer matrix [13].

A variety of antimicrobial compounds, encompassing metal-based complexes, organic small molecules, and nanomaterials, has been investigated for their ability to inhibit or eradicate harmful microbes[14–18]. Among these, nanomaterial can be included into packaging materials to enhance their antibacterial and antioxidant characteristics [19,20].

Carbon dots (CDs) exhibit unique optical characteristics, including robust UV absorption and tunable fluorescence, rendering them ideal for photoprotective applications[21]. Moreover, their extensive surface functionalities allow the efficient scavenging of free radicals, endowing significant antioxidant characteristics advantageous in both biomedical and packaging sectors. In comparison to commercially available UV absorbers, such as benzophenones or ZnO nanoparticles[22,23], CDs offer several advantages. They are synthesized from inexpensive, non-toxic precursors using a simple and scalable method. They exhibit excellent UV-blocking capabilities, minimizing concerns related to metal ion leaching or photocatalytic degradation. Additionally, CDs provide multifunctionality, including antioxidant and antibacterial properties, which are generally absent in traditional UV-blocking agents[24,25]. The reactivity, stability, and interaction of nanoparticles can be influenced by their size [26–30], which in turn affects their ability to preserve food quality and safety [31]. Surface functionality is a critical parameter in the design of materials intended for food packaging applications. The characteristics of packaging materials' surfaces can impact their interactions with food, including the absorption, barrier properties, and release of active compounds [32]. By precisely adjusting the surface properties, such as texture, water-repellence, and chemical makeup, it is feasible to enhance the effectiveness of packaging materials in terms of their ability to prevent the growth of microorganisms and preserve the freshness of products. MXenes are a type of nanostructured material that has recently gained attention. They are composed of two-dimensional transition metal carbides or nitrides [33,34]. MXenes possess several remarkable properties, such as high electrical conductivity, a high degree of mobility for charge carriers, numerous reactive spots, chemical resistance, decent biocompatibility, surface polarity, and the ability to modify surface groups [35]. By reducing the size of these 2D materials to structures smaller than 10 nm, such as MXene quantum dots (MQDs), their optical properties have been found to exhibit exceptional characteristics that have not been observed previously. These properties have led to the development of sensor applications [36]. Over the years, multiple reviews have been conducted to encompass the MXene-based sensors. Ullah et al. discussed in a the review focused on the overall sensing capabilities of MXene apatasensing [37]. Other reports have examined the use of fluorescent MQDs for detecting various analytes and for imaging applications [35,38,39]. Wang et al. conducted the first investigation of a smartphone-enabled ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor that utilizes blue/orange MQDs for the detection of histamine through fluorescence and visual means [40]. But still using MQDs in polymer film-based packaging is not a highly practiced area. Combination of MQDs in biopolymer could be an excellent alternative of sustainable food packing material.

Biocompatible macromolecules, such as polysaccharides derived from plants, are commonly used in the production of biopolymer packaging films because of their distinct physical characteristics and sustainable nature [41–43]. Chitosan is a cationic polysaccharide that is a natural biopolymer composed of glucosamine units. It is derived from the deacetylation of chitin, which is a polysaccharide called poly-β-(1,4)-N-acetyl-d-glucosamine [44]. Chitosan is primarily obtained from the exoskeletons of crustaceans such as shrimp, crab, and lobster. Chitosan production involves utilizing these shells, which are a byproduct of the seafood industry, as a means of waste utilization [45–49]. Some fungi have the ability to produce chitosan-like compounds that may have different properties compared to chitosan derived from crustaceans [50]. In addition, chitosan's notable coating/film developing capability has made it a valuable material for packaging [1,51], specifically as palatable films and coverings for fruits and produces. This application aims to broaden the shelf-life of unpreserved food stuffs [52]. However, chitosan possesses the disadvantage of underlying water interactions, which could result in unanticipated modifications to its chemistry and surface behaviours. This phenomenon is commonly detected in almost all kind of biopolymers [53]. Moreover, the chitosan derived films in their pure state demonstrate a notably elevated level of fragility and instability. The utilization of chitosan in conjunction with low molecular weight compounds has sparked considerable attention in modifying chitosan [43]. Plasticizers, which are diminutive additives, are frequently employed to augment the pliability of polymers. The occurrence of plasticization is often ascribed to different hypotheses, including the gelation, lubrication, and free volume theory [54]. This study presents the development of a multifunctional antibacterial film tailored for next-generation active packaging applications. By leveraging a one-pot thermal carbonization approach, MQDs were synthesized and subsequently integrated into a polymeric matrix via wet blending and precision molding techniques. The resulting nanocomposite films exhibit a synergistic combination of advanced functionalities, including potent antibacterial and antioxidant activities, enhanced mechanical robustness, efficient UV-shielding capabilities, and a tunable controlled release profile. Beyond laboratory evaluation, the films were systematically assessed for their efficacy in real-time fruit preservation scenarios and demonstrated prolonged shelf-life extension, confirming their suitability for practical deployment. To our knowledge, while some studies have examined polymer/MQD composites, none have investigated the composition of TPC with MQDs utilizing wet mixing followed by solution casting or a similar method. The integration of heteroatom-doped MQDs not only imparts multifunctionality but also offers a sustainable, scalable route toward intelligent packaging solutions that bridge the gap between material innovation and food preservation challenges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MXenes based quantum dots (MQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a flask, 5 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) and 0.6 g of lithium fluoride (LiF) were mixed with 5 mL of distilled water. The mix was agitated for 5 minutes using a magnetic A Teflon stir bar was used to fully dissolve the salt. Then, 1 g of Ti3AlC2 powder (MAX phase) was slowly added over 10 minutes to prevent the mixture from heating up too quickly, due to the exothermic nature of the reaction. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was incubated at a temperature of 40 °C for of 24 hours. Following this, the mixture had a thorough washing process consisting of 5–8 cycles including the addition of distilled water, centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 4 minutes per cycle, and decanting. This process was repeated until the pH of the supernatant reached around 6. The end product was diluted with a tiny quantity of deionized water. The suspension was flushed with nitrogen gas for 10 minutes, securely sealed, and thereafter subjected to ultrasonic waves in a bath sonicator for 5 hours [55]. The solution was then subjected to centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 4 minutes to separate individual Ti3C2Tx flakes from the remaining multilayer counterparts. Upon elimination of the solid particles, a uniform distribution of Ti3C2 nanoparticles in the liquid phase was achieved. The supernatant obtained was transferred to a new centrifuge tube, and 10 mL of deionized water was added for subsequent usage.

A total of 2 mL of Ti3C2 nanoparticles were distributed again in 35 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, 0.48 mL of PEI was introduced into the mixture and subjected to heating at 120 °C for 24 hours in the presence of a nitrogen environment. The resultant black suspension was passed through a 0.22 μm micro filter, resulting in the separation of a dark filtered solution. Subsequently, the colloidal solution underwent additional dialysis using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 4000 Da for duration of seven days, resulting in the production of Ti3C2 quantum dots (MQDs).

TPC is produced by dissolution of 3 g chitosan in 50 mL of 20 % aqueous glycerol solution, and subsequently drying the resultant solution [42]. Subsequently, we elevated the mixture's temperature to 65–70 °C and maintained it for duration of 1 hour, all the while ensuring constant stirring. The resultant of the reaction was a viscous, mucilaginous substance dispersed uniformly in the entirety of the liquid. The TPC technique was employed by putting the blended solution into Teflon Petridis, subsequently removing it by peeling, and then subjecting it to a drying process at 60 °C in oven for duration of 12 hours.

The TPC-based unfilled film was fabricated by a wet blending and solution casting method. Initially, approximately 0.5 g of TPC were dissolved in 20 milliliters of double-distilled water at ∼80 °C. Once all the constituents had achieved homogeneity (transparency), they were mixed together and vigorously stirred for a further hour at the same temperature. Next, the slurry obtained was put onto a Teflon petri dish and subjected to heating in a hot air oven at a temperature of 70 °C. In the case of MQD-based nanocomposite films, MQDs were incorporated into the TPC mixture, and the resulting mixture was agitated for 1 h for 85 °C to achieve full homogeneity. Subsequently, it was transferred into a Teflon petri dish and subjected to drying in a hot air oven at a temperature of around 70 °C. Once the Teflon petri dish had fully dried, the films (both control and without MQDs) were carefully removed and placed in a desiccator to prevent any more moisture absorption. The MQDs were blended with the polymer material in different ratios ranging from 1 % to 3 %. These blends were named TPCM1 (1 % MQDs loaded), TPCM2 (2 % MQDs loaded), and TPCM3 (3 % MQDs loaded).
Thermal analyses have been performed by TGA and DSC experiments. The methods are discussed in detail in the supporting information
The uniaxial tensile strength determination has been conducted also and the detailed method is included in the supporting information.
The biodegradation study has been conducted also and the detailed protocol is included in the supporting information.
A full cytotoxicity study protocol is available in the supplementary information.
Antioxidant tests were also conducted and included in supporting information.
The cytotoxicity assay via MTT test has been given in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of ethylene and CO2 production
ce:section.ce:para: The gas permeation assays have been given in the supporting information.

The MQDs were produced by one step thermal coupling approach as shown in
Fig. 1.

The UV–vis absorption spectra show two peaks at 310 nm, and 351 nm. These absorption peaks arise from their interband changeovers[56,57]. The excitation spectrum of MQDs shows an excitation band centered at 360 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b). Alike to the findings noticed with CDs and graphene quantum dots, the emission spectra of MQDs are generally broad and dependent on the excitation wavelength[58–60]. As the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 420 nm, the emission peaks gradually shift toward longer wavelengths. The most substantial peak appears around 485 nm when excited at 360 nm. The luminescence mechanism behind the excitation wavelength-dependent emissions from carbon-based quantum dots continues to be a subject of discussion. There are mainly two concepts: size effects and surface defects[61]. Fig. 3(c) illustrates the pH-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra of MQDs. The absorption intensity decreases by approximately 9 % as the pH value rises from 5 to 9. It is important to note that the pH of the MQDs solution was balanced using diluted HCl and NaOH. With increasing the pH, the surface attached amino groups of the MQDs become deprotonated, resulting in a decrease in their zeta potential, as revealed in Fig. 3(d). Usually, changes in pH lead to either protonation or deprotonation of the MQDs, causing significant alterations in the zeta potential of the nanoparticles. Fig. 3(e) presents the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of MQDs in aqueous dispersion at different pH levels. The PL intensity of the MQDs decreases as the pH value increases from 5 to 9. Though the emission intensity of the carbon nanoparticles remains relatively persistent in the neutral pH range, it typically experiences a decrease in PL intensity at both high and low pH levels due to deprotonation and protonation effects[62,63]. Fig. 3(f) illustrates the decay dynamic of dilute aqueous solution of MQDs under 390 nm light excitation. The average lifetime of MQDs was found to be 6.73 ns.

Fig. 3(a) displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Ti3AlC2 MAX phase, Ti3C2 nanoparticles, and MXenes based quantum dots. The pattern of Ti3C2 QDs shows the absence of the most intense peak at 39°, suggesting the elimination of interlayer Al following the etching of Ti3AlC2 with the etchant. Following the etching procedure, the majority of these diffraction peaks, such as those at (106), (109), and (116), were diminished. Additionally, the characteristic (002) peak in the Ti2C-MXene XRD pattern migrated towards a smaller angle as compared to Ti2AlC [64]. The monolayer structure of MQDs results in XRD patterns that are widened, have low intensity, and are tilted towards the lower angle side [65]. Conversely, the XRD pattern of the MQDs does not display any notable peaks. The lack of this phenomenon can be ascribed to the extremely small size of the MQDs, resulting in the broadening of peaks and a decrease in intensity due to the size broadening effect. Decreasing the size of particles results in a greater number of atoms on the surface; leading to an increase in lattice aberrations and a decrease in overall crystallinity [66]. In addition, the formation of quantum dots might result in a more disordered structure, lacking a well-defined arrangement across long distances, hence reducing the intensity of diffraction peaks.

Fig. 3(b) displays the FTIR spectra of Ti₃AlC₂, MXene, and MQDs. The spectra of Ti₃AlC₂ exhibits distinct absorption peaks at around 800 cm⁻¹ and 900 cm⁻¹ , which correspond to the vibrations of Ti-O and Ti-C, respectively. After undergoing exfoliation to create MXene, new bands become visible, especially in the range of 1000 cm⁻¹ to 2000 cm⁻¹ , which suggests the existence of surface functional groups that were added during the exfoliation procedure. The spectrum of MXene exhibits a wide peak at approximately 3400 cm⁻¹ , which indicates the presence of O-H stretching vibrations. Additionally, there is another peak around 1600 cm⁻¹ , which corresponds to H-O-H bending vibrations. These findings suggest that there are water molecules adsorbed in the MXene. The MQDs exhibit a variety of new peaks and strengthened bands in the FTIR spectrum, indicating the presence of different functional groups. The bands observed at around 3200–3500 cm⁻¹ are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H, which suggest the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface. The peaks observed at approximately 2900 cm⁻¹ are indicative of C-H stretching vibrations, whilst the bands observed around 1650 cm⁻¹ can be attributed to N-H bending vibrations. The existence of Ti-O peaks at approximately 600 cm⁻¹ provides additional evidence that certain structural patterns of the MXene material have been preserved.

Fig. 3(c) demonstrates the Raman spectra of Ti₃AlC₂ and MXene Quantum Dots (MQDs). The spectrum of Ti₃AlC₂ displays several peaks: ω₁ at roughly 200 cm⁻¹ , ω₂ at around 400 cm⁻¹ , ω₃ near 600 cm⁻¹ , and ω₄ at about 800 cm⁻¹ . The presence of these peaks in the Ti₃AlC₂ crystal structure indicates the vibrations associated with the Ti-C and Al layers. Conversely, the Raman spectra of the MQDs exhibit a diffuse and feeble signal lacking clearly defined peaks. The absence of notable Raman peaks can be ascribed to the diminutive dimensions and elevated surface-to-volume ratio of the quantum dots, resulting in less crystallinity and increased structural disorder in comparison to the bulk material. The lack of distinct peaks in the MQD spectrum indicates that the quantum dots no longer possess the extended arrangement found in the Ti₃AlC₂ precursor, thereby confirming their nanostructured nature and possibly indicating a more disordered state. The observed structural change aligns with the findings of XRD and FTIR investigations, indicating a shift from a highly organized crystal structure to a less organized quantum dot system.

Fig. 3(d) depicts the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of MQDs. Fig. 3(d) displays the XPS survey spectrum of MQDs, revealing the presence of various essential elements: carbon (C1s) at approximately 285 eV, nitrogen (N1s) at around 400 eV, titanium (Ti2p) at approximately 460 eV, oxygen (O1s) at around 530 eV, and fluorine (F1s) at approximately 685 eV. The presence of these peaks confirms the elemental composition of the MQDs, providing evidence that functional groups and elements were successfully incorporated during the synthesis process. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3(e) exhibits distinct peaks that correspond to various carbon-containing functional groups. The presence of the peak at 284.8 eV is associated with the C-C/C-H bonds, which indicate the presence of sp² hybridized carbon in the MQDs. The peak observed at 285.7 eV is attributed to the presence of C-N bonds, indicating the existence of carbon structures that have been doped with nitrogen. The peaks observed at 286.5 eV and 288.5 eV are indicative of the presence of C-O and CO bonds, respectively, on the surface of the MQD. These peaks suggest the existence of oxygenated functional groups. The O1s spectrum in Fig. 3(f) exhibits distinct peaks at 529.7 eV and 531.5 eV, indicating high resolution. The peak detected at 529.7 eV is ascribed to the existence of titanium oxide species, representing the existence of Ti-O bonds. The peak observed at 531.5 eV implies to the occurrence of hydroxyl functionalities bonded to titanium in the C-Ti-(OH)x compound. These hydroxyl groups can improve the hydrophilicity and functionality of the MQDs. The Ti2p spectrum in Fig. 3(g) exhibits distinct peaks that correspond to various titanium chemical states, with a high level of detail. The peaks observed at 458.6 eV and 464.5 eV correspond to the Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 orbitals of TiO₂, respectively, suggesting the presence of titanium dioxide. The peaks observed at 455.2 eV and 461.0 eV are indicative of the existence of Ti-C bonds, thus providing confirmation of the presence of titanium-carbide structures in the MQDs. Moreover, the presence of peaks at 459.0 eV and 465.0 eV suggests the existence of Ti-F bonds, indicating the presence of fluorinated titanium species. These species are likely formed as a result of the etching process employed during the synthesis of MQDs. The XPS analysis of MQDs demonstrates the existence of diverse functional groups and elements, such as carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, titanium, and fluorine. This indicates the successful synthesis and functionalization of MXene Quantum Dots. The thorough analysis of the C1s, O1s, and Ti2p spectra reveals the intricate chemical surroundings and the existence of multiple bonding states, which can contribute to the distinctive characteristics and potential uses of MQDs in diverse fields.

Fig. 3(d) demonstrates the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of MQDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3d) indicates the existence of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), titanium (Ti2p), nitrogen (N1s), and fluorine (F1s) within the MQDs. The identified specific binding energies are approximately 285 eV for C1s, 530 eV for O1s, 460 eV for Ti2p, 400 eV for N1s, and 685 eV for F1s. The C1s spectrum; with high resolution (Fig. 3e), exhibits multiple peaks that signify distinct carbon chemical states. The peak observed at 284.8 eV is indicative of C-C bonds, which implies the existence of sp² hybridized carbon. This type of carbon is commonly found in graphitic structures. The peak observed at 285.6 eV is ascribed to the existence of C-N bonds, which suggests the presence of carbon that has been doped with nitrogen. The peak observed at 286.6 eV corresponds to the presence of C-O bonds, which are typically associated with alcohol or ether functional groups. The peak at 288.5 eV is attributed to CO bonds, which suggest the presence of carbonyl or carboxyl groups. Within the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3f), the process of peak deconvolution uncovers two primary constituents. The peak observed at 530.8 eV is ascribed to the existence of titanium oxides, indicating the presence of Ti-O bonds. The peak observed at 532.5 eV signifies the presence of C-Ti-(OH)x bonds, which indicates the attachment of hydroxyl groups to titanium. The Ti2p spectrum, depicted in Fig. 3(g), exhibits multiple peaks, which suggest the presence of various titanium oxidation states and bonding environments. The peak observed at 458.6 eV corresponds to the presence of Ti-O bonds in TiO2, whereas the peak observed at 463.2 eV is specifically associated with the Ti2p3/2 state of TiO2. The peaks observed at 454.6 eV and 461.1 eV correspond to the presence of Ti-C and Ti-C-Ti-F bonds, respectively, indicating the existence of titanium carbide and titanium fluoride species. The XPS analysis provides conclusive evidence of the intricate chemical composition of the MQDs, revealing various bonding configurations for carbon, oxygen, and titanium, along with the existence of nitrogen and fluorine functionalities.

The Fig. 4(a) and (b) plots display the FTIR spectra of chitosan, and other synthesized films loaded with MQDs. These spectra clearly demonstrate notable changes in the structure resulting from the inclusion of MQDs. Fig. 4(a) shows that the chitosan sample displays prominent absorption bands at 3447 cm−1 (O-H stretching), 1657 cm−1 (amide I), and other unique peaks that indicate its molecular structure. During thermoplastic modification, significant changes occur in the infrared spectrum. The O-H stretching band shifts to 2875 cm−1, a new peak at 1722 cm−1 corresponding to CO stretching emerges, and additional peaks at 1150 cm−1 and 1014 cm−1 are detected. These observations suggest the formation of ester and other functional groups as a result of the thermoplastic processing. The FTIR spectra of the TPCM samples (TPCM1, TPCM2, TPCM3) in Fig. 4(b) show additional changes as a result of different amounts of MQDs. The peaks associated with the TPC matrix are preserved, but, there are discernible alterations resulting from the interaction with MQDs. The O-H and N-H stretching bands in TPCM1 exhibit a small drop in intensity, indicating an early contact with MQDs. With the rise in MQDs content (TPCM2 and TPCM3), more significant changes may be noticed. The peak at 3447 cm−1 is further displaced and expanded, showing an increase in hydrogen bonding interactions between chitosan and MQDs. Furthermore, the CO stretching peak at 1722 cm−1 becomes more prominent, indicating a significant contact and potentially some covalent bonding between the polymer matrix and MQDs. The peaks observed at 1150 cm−1 and 1014 cm−1 exhibit changes in both intensity and position, providing evidence for the development of a cohesive composite structure. The observed spectrum changes confirm the effective addition of MQDs into the thermoplastic chitosan matrix, leading to altered chemical surroundings and potential enhancements in mechanical and functional properties.

The water content of the neat chitosan, TPC, and MQDs loaded TPC (TPCM) films, as shown in Fig. 4(c), provides the information about the hydrophilicity of these materials. The water content of neat chitosan is around 6 %, which aligns with its hydrophilic characteristics resulting from the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups. After undergoing thermoplastic modification, the water content experiences a substantial increase to over 10 %. This suggests an improved hydrophilicity, which is likely a result of the incorporation of extra hydrophilic groups during the thermoplastic process. The water content fluctuates based on the concentration of MQDs when they are included. TPCM1 exhibits a marginal reduction in water content, reaching around 9 %. This indicates that the initial inclusion of MQDs somewhat counteracts the hydrophilic effects caused by thermoplastic modification. As the MQD content increases in TPCM2 and TPCM3, the water content drops to around 8 % and 7 %, respectively. The observed pattern suggests that increased levels of MQDs decrease the overall hydrophilicity of the composite films, potentially because of the hydrophobic properties of MXenes or the origination of a denser structure that restricts water absorption.

Fig. 4(d) exhibits the thermal degradation curves of the pure and different hybrid TPC films. The sample films exhibited a consistent thermal degradation pattern, characterised by a gradual decrease in weight at around 120 °C, followed by a significant mass loss between 180 and 350°C. The initial weight loss at around 120 °C was attributed to the evaporation of moisture present in the polymer films. The weight loss of the glycerol plasticized film exceeds that of the neat chitosan film, indicating a higher water content in the former. The findings indicate that the inclusion of glycerol enhanced the ability of the chitosan film to absorb moisture, as glycerol possesses a strong affinity for water. The significant decrease in weight observed between temperatures of 180 and 350°C can be assigned to a complicated procedure in which both the acetylated and deacetylated units of chitosan and glycerol undergo decomposition. The incorporation of MQDs into the TPC films affects the thermal stability of films. The magnified image at Fig. 4(e) shows the onset of degradation and residue amount, respectively. The residue is showing gradual increment with the incorporation of MQDs implying their improvement in thermal stability. The glass transition temperature (Tg) is a critical characteristic of polymers, at which their physical properties experience substantial alterations. An investigation was conducted to examine the impact of glycerol and MQDs on the Tg of chitosan films. Fig. 4(f) displays the DSC thermograms of both the neat and TPC based hybrid films. The glass transition temperature of the pure chitosan film was determined to be 60 °C. This temperature closely matched the previously reported Tg values of approximately 55 °C for chitosan films [67]. The addition of plasticizer resulted in a decrease in the Tg from 60 °C for the pure chitosan to −27°C for glycerol. But after incorporation of MQDs, the Tg shows a right shift towards higher temperature. For TPCM1, the change is not prominent but for TPCM2 and TPCM3 the Tg shift occur ∼3–5 °C which could infer their low temperature flexibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical strength related studies of TPC hybrid films
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical characteristics of polymer films augmented with MXene quantum dots based on TPC, as shown in the provided graphs, demonstrate substantial enhancements in comparison to pure chitosan. Fig. 4(g) illustrates the stress-strain curve, indicating that pure chitosan achieves a peak stress at fracture of around 30 MPa. Nonetheless, it exhibits a constrained elongation at break (EB), indicating its brittle nature. The incorporation of MQDs in the analysis of TPC and TPCMs results in a notable reduction in brittleness. The TPC sample demonstrates a decrease in ultimate stress (about 21 MPa) relative to pure chitosan, yet shows a significant increase in elongation at break to around 4 %, indicating improved flexibility. The TPCM samples, especially TPCM2, demonstrate an optimal balance between strength and elongation, with TPCM2 achieving a stress level of approximately 26 MPa and an elongation at break of about 3.5 %. The findings demonstrate that the application of MQDs enhances mechanical properties by promoting superior load distribution and preventing crack propagation.

Fig. 4(h) provides additional clarity on these mechanical improvements by comparing the TS and EB among various samples through the use of a histogram. The chitosan has a high tensile strength (about 30 MPa) but a poor elongation at break (around 1.5 %). A noticeable displacement occurs upon the introduction of MQDs, as evidenced in the TPC and TPCM samples. The TPC exhibits a mild tensile strength of approximately 23 MPa, accompanied by a significantly higher elongation at break of around 4 %. Among the reinforced samples, TPCM2 is distinguished by its superior tensile strength (about 28 MPa) and notable elongation at break (around 3.5 %), reflecting its exceptional mechanical properties. TPCM1 and TPCM3 demonstrate superior tensile strength (∼24 MPa) and elongation at break (about 2–3 %) relative to pure chitosan, however not to the same degree as TPCM2. The enhancement of mechanical qualities can be attributed to the uniform distribution of MQDs inside the polymer matrix. This enables more efficient transfer of stress and strengthens the link between the chitosan matrix and the MQDs fillers. The results emphasize the capacity of MQDs to greatly enhance the strength and flexibility of TPC hybrid films, making them highly promising materials for applications that demand high strength and flexibility.

Incorporating UV-blocking capabilities into packaging films is critical for protecting light-sensitive food products from photodegradation, which can lead to discoloration, nutrient loss, and spoilage. UV radiation, particularly in the UV-A and UV-B ranges, accelerates oxidative reactions and microbial growth, thereby compromising food quality and shelf life. The integration of quantum dots, known for their strong UV-absorption and photostability, offers a sustainable strategy to mitigate these effects while enhancing the multifunctionality of the packaging material. The UV–visible spectra of TPC/MQDs hybrid films are depicted in Fig. 5(a), demonstrating their capacity to block incident UV radiation. The TPC film, in its original state, demonstrates excellent light transmission in both the UV and visible range, suggesting that it has a limited capacity to block UV radiation. On the other hand, films containing MQDs (TPCM1, TPCM2, TPCM3) exhibit a notable decrease in transmittance specifically in the UV region. This reduction becomes more prominent as the amount of MQDs in the films increases. This indicates that the MQDs efficiently absorb or disperse UV light, thereby improving the film's ability to protect against damaging UV radiation. Fig. 5(b) demonstrates the quantification of the UV-blocking capability. It reveals that the TPC film alone provides a moderate level of protection against UVA and UVB rays. However, the inclusion of MQDs significantly improves this characteristic. TPCM3, when loaded with the highest MQDs, exhibits superior UV blocking capabilities, with UVA and UVB blocking percentages nearly twice as high as those of the neat TPC film. Compared to conventional UV-blocking agents such as titanium dioxide (TiO₂), zinc oxide (ZnO), or organic UV absorbers (e.g., benzophenones or benzotriazoles), the MQDs used in this study offer several key advantages [68]. First, MQDs are derived from carbon-rich precursors through a green and scalable synthesis, making them more environmentally benign and biocompatible. Second, the doping of heteroatoms enhances their electronic structure, resulting in broader and stronger absorption in the UV region, which translates to superior UV-shielding performance. Third, unlike metal oxide-based blockers, MQDs do not pose risks of photocatalytic degradation or potential toxicity due to nanoparticle leaching. Moreover, their intrinsic fluorescence and antioxidant properties contribute synergistically to the film’s multifunctionality, making them ideal candidates for intelligent and active packaging systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas barrier property, wettability and biodegradability
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5(c) displays the OTR of the films, which is a vital metric for packaging purposes. The OTR quantifies the extent to which oxygen can penetrate the material. The TPC film in its original state exhibits the highest OTR value, indicating that it is comparatively more permeable to oxygen. Introducing MQDs leads to a notable decrease in the OTR, with TPCM3 exhibiting the most minimal OTR value. The decrease in OTR as the content of MQDs increases suggests that MQDs create a more complex route for oxygen molecules, thereby improving the gas barrier characteristics of the films. This enhancement is crucial for applications that necessitate extended shelf life and decreased oxidation.

Fig. 5(d)-(g) exhibit digital photographs of the films, showcasing their visual characteristics. The initial TPC film is characterized by its clarity and transparency, whereas the films containing MQDs exhibit a growing level of opaqueness as the amount of MQDs increases. This suggests the presence of scattered MQDs within the film's structure. Fig. 5(h)-(k) depict the films when exposed to UV irradiation with a wavelength of 365 nm. The TPC film in its original state does not display any fluorescence, whereas the films loaded with MQDs exhibit vibrant fluorescence that becomes more pronounced as the amount of MQDs increases.

Fig. 5(l) displays the WVTR of the films, which quantifies the speed at which water vapor permeates through the film. The TPC film is characterized by its exceptional WVTR, which signifies a greater susceptibility to water vapor permeation. The inclusion of MQDs in the TPC matrix reduces the WVTR, with TPCM3 exhibiting the most minimal rate. This indicates that the MQDs function as obstacles to the diffusion of water vapor, thereby improving the moisture barrier characteristics of the film. This is advantageous for packaging purposes. Fig. 5(m) displays the water contact angles of the films. The chitosan with a neat structure exhibits the highest contact angle, which suggests a greater hydrophobic characteristic. The TPC film exhibits a reduced contact angle, suggesting a higher degree of hydrophilicity. The presence of MQDs enhances the contact angle, with TPCM3 exhibiting the highest contact angle among the films loaded with MQDs. This suggests that MQDs enhance the hydrophobicity of the surface, which can be beneficial in situations where water repellency is required.

Fig. 5(n) illustrates the biodegradability of the TPCM3 film in the presence and absence of lysozyme in PBS. The weight loss percentage over time reflects the rate at which the film degrades. When lysozyme is present, the film undergoes accelerated degradation, resulting in noticeable reduction in weight within a span of 20 days. In the absence of lysozyme, the degradation of the film occurs at a slower rate, suggesting that the presence of enzymatic activity enhances the speed of the biodegradation process. This property is essential for eco-friendly applications, as it guarantees that the material cans biodegrade naturally over time without leaving any toxic residues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant of the MQDs loaded hybrid films
ce:section.ce:para: An assessment was carried out to evaluate the antioxidant properties of hybrid films loaded with MQDs employing DPPH and ABTS assays. The antioxidant features of the films containing MQDs vary based on the concentration of the MQDs used. Known for their strong antioxidant capabilities, QDs' effectiveness is regulated by their origin and the methods used for their preparation. In the DPPH and ABTS assays, the TPC specimen showed < 3 % activeness in capturing free radicals. However, the addition of MQDs resulted in a significant increase in the DPPH and ABTS scavenging efficacy of the nanocomposite films, as illustrated in Figs. 6a and 6(b). The high antioxidant activity of the MQDs is attributed to the abundance of electron-donating functional groups on their surface, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other components. The incorporation of MQDs led to the development of a composite film that demonstrated improved antioxidant properties compared to pure TPC. The determinations point that the existence of MQDs significantly raises the ability to scavenge free radicals. Antioxidant materials play a crucial role in food wrapping, as they help avoid the oxidation of food items, which can lead to degeneration, off-flavors, loss of alimentary values, and the generation of toxic chemicals. Antioxidants prolong food shelf life by minimizing oxidation, thereby preserving its superiority and longevity. The produced MQDs in food packaging render supplementary benefits owing to their intrinsic antioxidant quality. They efficiently neutralize free radicals, enhancing the oxidative constancy of foodstuff. Additionally, MQDs exhibit biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and environmental friendliness, rendering them a safer substitute for artificial antioxidants. Their versatile functionality, encompassing antibacterial capabilities and photoluminescence, offers additional advantages, including the facilitation of real-time food quality monitoring. The comparisons have been made with recently reported antioxidant quantum dots and shown in Table 1.

Antimicrobial activity is a crucial attribute employed in assorted active food packaging engineering sectors. An assessment was conducted to assess the efficiency of MQDs-based nanocomposite films (TPCM3) in presence of foodborne pathogenic bacteria, specifically L. monocytogenes and E. coli, as illustrated in Figs. 6c and 6(d). Chitosan demonstrated antibacterial activity, indicating a notable bacteriostatic outcome due to the inherent antimicrobial behaviors of the control film. The incorporation of MQDs into the polymeric matrix markedly enhanced antibacterial efficiency against both E. coli and L. monocytogenes (Figs. 6c and 6d).

Freshly cultivated E. coli and L. monocytogenes cells were distinctly subjected to the hybrid films and incubated at 37 °C with constant agitation. Subsequent to the incubation period, analysis of the bacterial cells exposed to the TPCD3 film demonstrated that these films are exceptionally proficient in suppressing bacterial proliferation. Conversely, the control sample for both bacterial strains demonstrated substantial growth. The findings correspond with earlier research indicating that nanocomposites infused with CDs exhibit significant antibacterial efficacy against L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Travlou et al. examined the antibacterial efficacy of ‘S’ and ‘N’ co-doped carbon dots against E. coli and B. subtilis. [69]. Their findings indicated that the physicochemical properties and nanoscale dimensions of the carbon dots impact their antibacterial effectiveness. Specifically, the occurrence of amide functional groups and amino groups on the surface of the carbon dots plays a substantial role in improving antibacterial activity. The positively charged nature of these groups facilitates electrostatic interactions with the phospholipids in the bacterial cell membrane, ultimately leading to necrobiosis. The primary nitrogen species are anticipated hydrogen ion addition, as indicated by the +ve surface charges of the carbon dots. Additionally, the surface functionalities are activated to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and superoxide ions, contributing to their antimicrobial effects. ROS, including O2 –, OH–, and HO2 –, are known to damage cellular elements such as nucleotides, lipids, proteins, and metabolites, finally leading to cell death [69,70]. The binding agent of MQDs, TPC, demonstrates considerable antibacterial activities, principally attributable to the characteristic features of chitosan. The antibacterial mechanistic pathways entail the split of bacterial cell walls via interactions between the cationic amine functionalities of chitosan and the anionic constituents of bacterial cell membranes. This interaction results in cell membrane breakdown and the discharge of intracellular components. Furthermore, chitosan can complex with essential metallic ions, so inhibiting the function of bacterial enzymes and DNA replication. These films function as efficient barriers, inhibiting bacterial adhesion and the development of biofilms. This method effectively prevents both gram +ve and -ve bacteria, rendering it appropriate for usage in food wrapping and external coatings to limit microbial proliferation [71].

The inclusion of antibacterial compounds into food packaging is essential for improving food safety, as it effectively inhibits the progression of toxic bacteria and subsequently lowers the risk of foodborne illnesses. These compounds prolong the shelf-life of food products by preventing bacterial growth, thereby preserving nutritional feature and minimizing foodstuff left-over. Furthermore, these materials facilitate a decrease in chemical preservatives, in accordance with customer preferences for natural and organic produces. Moreover, antimicrobial packaging enhances purchaser self-reliance, offers market differentiation, and assists in adhering to rigorous food safety regulations. Antibacterial compounds enhance food protection, durability, and quality, benefiting consumers and the environment. The observed biological activities of the MQDs, including antioxidant and antibacterial effects, can be linked to their unique structural and chemical features. The small size of the MQDs provides a high surface-to-volume ratio, enhancing their interaction with microbial membranes and reactive species. The presence of surface functional groups introduced through PEI modification such as amine groups plays a crucial role in disrupting bacterial cell walls and scavenging free radicals. These groups can interact electrostatically with negatively charged bacterial membranes, leading to membrane destabilization and cell death. Also, these surface functional groups act as active sites for electron or hydrogen donation, facilitating the neutralization of DPPH and ABTS⁺• radicals. MQDs provide benefits over metal oxide-based antibacterial agents for biocompatibility, reduced toxicity, and enhanced environmental protection, delivering them more suitable for food-based practical usages. In contrast to metal oxides, these QDs do not provide toxicological problems or environmental issues. The biological activity of MQDs is a result of the combined effects of their small size, and surface chemistry, making them suitable for use in active food packaging. Their facile fabrication, economic viability, and significance for environmentally benign degradation augment their attractiveness as sophisticated antibacterial agents for food packaging.

The antibacterial and antioxidant capabilities of MQDs loaded hydrogels prompted us to explore their possible utility in food preservation. Produced hydrogel films are ideal for food product preservation because to their high transparency, antimicrobial, and antioxidant characteristics. Bananas were covered with hydrogel films and photographed at regular intervals during the storage time ( Fig. 7). As illustrated in Fig. 7, bananas had a bright yellow color and perfect exteriors on the initial day. But as time passed in storage, they grew ripe and then went bad. The identification of marked and darkened regions serves as an example of this. Bananas coated with hydrogel that included MQDs exhibited reduced deterioration compared to those uncoated samples. Results suggest that the nanocomposites may enhance fruit storage and increase the longevity of the fruit due to its excellent antioxidant characteristics.

The release kinetics of MQDs from the film matrix directly influences the performance and longevity of the film’s active functions, particularly in food monitoring systems. A controlled and sustained release ensures prolonged antibacterial and antioxidant activity, which is essential for extending food shelf life and maintaining product integrity [75,76]. Conversely, uncontrolled or rapid leaching may lead to premature depletion of the functional agents, reducing the protective efficacy of the film over time. Furthermore, for intelligent packaging applications, the presence of MQDs in the food-contact environment must be tightly regulated to ensure biocompatibility and safety [77]. Fig. 8 provides a comprehensive overview of the functional performance of TPC/MQDs nanocomposite films by showing the release kinetics of MQDs and their biocompatibility. Fig. 8(a), (b), and (c) show the cumulative release profiles of MQDs from TPCM1, TPCM2, and TPCM3 nanocomposite films, respectively. A graph illustrating the total amount of substance released over time is plotted for various solvents, including water, 10 % ethanol, 50 % ethanol, and 95 % ethanol. The total distribution of the three films is greatest in water, then 10 % ethanol, followed by 50 % ethanol, and finally 95 % ethanol. More precisely, the total amount released after 10 hours in water is approximately 35 % for TPCM1, 40 % for TPCM2, and 45 % for TPCM3. When dissolved in a solution containing 10 % ethanol, the values decrease slightly to approximately 30 %, 35 %, and 40 % respectively. The release of MQDs from the films is considerably reduced when using 50 % ethanol and 95 % ethanol, with cumulative release percentages of approximately 20 % and 15 % respectively. This suggests that solvents with higher polarity promote a faster release of MQDs. The release profiles indicate that the loading of MQDs has an impact on the rate of release, with a higher loading (TPCM3) leading to a greater cumulative release. The cumulative release profiles demonstrate how the release rates of MQDs are affected by the polarity of the solvent. The release rate is directly proportional to the polarity of the solvent, as demonstrated by the highest release in water and the lowest release in 95 % ethanol. This implies that in water-based environments, the films can efficiently transport MQDs, making them appropriate for applications that necessitate rapid release.

Fig. 8(d-f) display the Korsmeyer-Peppas model fittings of the release data for TPCM1, TPCM2, and TPCM3, correspondingly. The governing equation for this fitting has been given as follows:
(1)

The variable F(D) denotes the amount of medications provided at a specific time 't'. The variable 'k' remains constant in this context. The constant kKP encompasses the physical and arithmetical attributes of the release system like their surface morphology, domain, and width. The release exponent, represented by n, defines the release mechanism. The variable n yields crucial comprehensions into the pathways by which medicines are discharged from CDs. The magnitude of n regulates whether the release is predominantly mediated via diffusion through the nanoparticles-based matrices, surface disruption, or a blending of both mechanisms. The fitted curves demonstrate that the release process adheres to a non-Fickian diffusion mechanism, indicating an amalgamation of diffusion and erosion-controlled release. The ‘n’ values for TPCM1, TPCM2, and TPCM3 in different food simulants range from 0.47 to 0.82, providing additional evidence for the anomalous transport mechanism. Fig. 8(g) displays the degree to which MQDs are enclosed within the TPCM1, TPCM2, and TPCM3 films when exposed to various food simulant fluids. The samples exhibit the highest encapsulation efficiency when placed in 95 % ethanol, while the lowest encapsulation efficiency is observed when placed in water. More precisely, the encapsulation efficiencies for TPCM3 are approximately 80 % in 95 % ethanol, 75 % in 50 % ethanol, 70 % in 10 % ethanol, and 65 % in water. TPCM2 and TPCM1 exhibit a comparable pattern, albeit with marginally reduced efficiencies. This suggests that MQDs exhibit greater stability and improved encapsulation in environments with lower polarity. The data indicates that TPCM3 consistently exhibits the highest encapsulation efficiency, which is consistent with its higher MQDs content. This higher content of MQDs provides more active sites for encapsulation. Fig. 8(h) displays the outcomes of a cytotoxicity assay that assesses the compatibility of the nanocomposite films by measuring the survival of cells over a period of 5 days. The viability of cells in all three films (TPCM1, TPCM2, and TPCM3) remained consistently high, ranging from 95 % to 100 %, which is comparable to the control, throughout the entire testing period. This suggests that the films are not harmful and compatible with living organisms. It is imperative for prospective biomedical uses that the films do not pose any harm to living cells and can be safely employed in medical settings.

Fig. 9(a) illustrates the mechanism of the composite films developed for fruit preservation. The CS-TA/MXene composite films enhance the fruit's shelf life by minimizing weight loss caused by superior water vapor and oxygen barrier properties. Furthermore, the composite films contain amino and phenolic hydroxyl groups that possess antibacterial properties, effectively preventing bacterial infection and extending the shelf life of the fruits. Simultaneously, the MXene sheet, along with the benzene ring and phenolic hydroxyl group, acts as a barrier against oxygen and ultraviolet light. This effectively decreases the oxidation and degradation of the fruit, resulting in a longer shelf life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ethylene (C2H4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) evolution
ce:section.ce:para: The banana fruit, subjected to ethylene treatment in marketable conveniences, exhibited a distinct maturing convention when warehoused at a temperature of 20 °C and a RH of 80–85 %. Significant fluctuations in the yields of C2H4 and CO2 were seen in banana that were exposed to C2H4 and had the maturing process begin. These alterations were consistently seen throughout the gas production period. The ethylene gas output exhibited a consistent upward trend, commencing on day 0 and culminating on day 2, attaining a peak value of 0.55 ng/kg/s. Afterwards, the production of ethylene remained stable until day 8 and then experienced a substantial increase during subsequent evolution. CO2 production reached its highest level at a maximum value of 30.94 µg/kg/s on the fourth day. The production rates were rather constant during both the maturing and over- maturing stages, as depicted in Fig. 9(b,c).

In summary, we successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped MQDs using a simple and optimized hydrothermal method. The fluorescent MQDs were thoroughly characterized using spectroscopic techniques and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to confirm their elemental composition. A detailed investigation was conducted to assess the water absorbency behavior of the TPC-based films. The modified nanocomposite films incorporating TPC demonstrated excellent mechanical properties, exhibiting flexibility and a tensile strength of approximately 28 MPa under uniaxial tension. Electron microscopy further confirmed the film’s permeability characteristics, with oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) measured at approximately 39.02 cm³ ·μm/(m²·day·kPa) and 599.4 g/m²·24 h, respectively. Additionally, the TPC films effectively restricted the penetration of UVA and UVB radiation, with this protective effect becoming more pronounced as the concentration of MQDs within the TPC matrix increased. The radical scavenging activity of the TPC films was examined to confirm their antioxidant capabilities. TPC films infused with MQDs displayed significant antioxidant activity, achieving over 75 % inhibition in both DPPH and ABTS experiments. In practical assessment, bananas covered in the nanocomposite thin films maintained their visual integrity for a minimum of five days. The results underscore the new material's potential as a viable and ecological alternative for food preservation and packaging applications. This present study highlights the multifunctional properties of MQDs, prompting future research to prioritize the development of energy-efficient and cost-effective synthesis methods to enhance commercial viability. Moreover, additional research on long-term stability, biodegradation kinetics in real-world conditions, and compatibility with various food types will be essential to regulatory approval and practical use. The integration of the nanocomposite film into existing food packaging systems and an assessment of its performance in extended supply networks may provide new opportunities for sustainable packaging solutions.
